questions.question: When a switch learns a MAC address, which address does it record — source or destination?
questions.explanation: Switches learn by reading the SOURCE MAC address and the port it arrived on.

correct.explanation: Switches learn by reading the SOURCE MAC address and the port it arrived on.

incorrect.explanation: "Destination MAC of the frame" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Switches learn by reading the SOURCE MAC address and the port it arrived on.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Both source and destination" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Switches learn by reading the SOURCE MAC address and the port it arrived on.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Neither — only IP addresses" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Switches learn by reading the SOURCE MAC address and the port it arrived on.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

questions.question: A frame arrives whose destination MAC IS in the MAC address table, mapped to a different port than it arrived on. What does the switch do?
choices: Forward it out only the mapped port
questions.explanation: A known unicast destination is forwarded out only the one port it is mapped to.

correct.explanation: A known unicast destination is forwarded out only the one port it is mapped to.

incorrect.explanation: "Flood it out all ports" is incorrect because the scenario requires: A known unicast destination is forwarded out only the one port it is mapped to.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Drop the frame" is incorrect because the scenario requires: A known unicast destination is forwarded out only the one port it is mapped to.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Broadcast it within the VLAN" is incorrect because the scenario requires: A known unicast destination is forwarded out only the one port it is mapped to.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Choosing a plausible command that does not solve this specific scenario

questions.question: What does a switch do with a frame whose destination MAC is NOT in its MAC address table?
choices: Sends it back to the source
Floods it out all ports in the VLAN except the source port
Sends it to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast frames are flooded out every port in the same VLAN except the ingress port.

correct.explanation: Unknown unicast frames are flooded out every port in the same VLAN except the ingress port.

incorrect.explanation: "Drops it" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Unknown unicast frames are flooded out every port in the same VLAN except the ingress port.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Sends it back to the source" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Unknown unicast frames are flooded out every port in the same VLAN except the ingress port.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Sends it to the default gateway" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Unknown unicast frames are flooded out every port in the same VLAN except the ingress port.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

questions.question: What is the default MAC address table aging time on a Cisco switch?
questions.explanation: The default aging time is 300 seconds (5 minutes) of inactivity.

correct.explanation: The default aging time is 300 seconds (5 minutes) of inactivity.

questions.question: PC-A and PC-B are both connected to the same switch port via a hub. PC-A sends a frame to PC-B. What does the switch do?
choices: Forwards it out a different port
Filters it — does not forward, since both are on the same port
questions.explanation: If source and destination MAC map to the same ingress port, the switch filters (does not forward) the frame.

correct.explanation: If source and destination MAC map to the same ingress port, the switch filters (does not forward) the frame.

incorrect.explanation: "Forwards it out a different port" is incorrect because the scenario requires: If source and destination MAC map to the same ingress port, the switch filters (does not forward) the frame.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Floods it everywhere" is incorrect because the scenario requires: If source and destination MAC map to the same ingress port, the switch filters (does not forward) the frame.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "Drops it as an error" is incorrect because the scenario requires: If source and destination MAC map to the same ingress port, the switch filters (does not forward) the frame.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

questions.question: What MAC address represents a Layer 2 broadcast?
questions.explanation: FFFF.FFFF.FFFF is the Ethernet broadcast address; switches always flood broadcasts within the VLAN.

correct.explanation: FFFF.FFFF.FFFF is the Ethernet broadcast address; switches always flood broadcasts within the VLAN.

incorrect.explanation: "0000.0000.0000" is incorrect because the scenario requires: FFFF.FFFF.FFFF is the Ethernet broadcast address; switches always flood broadcasts within the VLAN.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "0000.0000.FFFF" is incorrect because the scenario requires: FFFF.FFFF.FFFF is the Ethernet broadcast address; switches always flood broadcasts within the VLAN.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "AAAA.AAAA.AAAA" is incorrect because the scenario requires: FFFF.FFFF.FFFF is the Ethernet broadcast address; switches always flood broadcasts within the VLAN.

questions.question: True or False: A switch may flood a frame out a port that is in a different VLAN than the frame.
questions.explanation: False — flooding is scoped per-VLAN; a frame is never flooded into a different VLAN.

correct.explanation: False — flooding is scoped per-VLAN; a frame is never flooded into a different VLAN.

incorrect.explanation: "True" is incorrect because the scenario requires: False — flooding is scoped per-VLAN; a frame is never flooded into a different VLAN.

questions.question: Which command displays the switch MAC address table?
questions.explanation: `show mac address-table` lists learned (and static) MAC-to-port mappings.

correct.explanation: `show mac address-table` lists learned (and static) MAC-to-port mappings.

incorrect.explanation: "show ip interface brief" is incorrect because the scenario requires: `show mac address-table` lists learned (and static) MAC-to-port mappings.

incorrect.explanation: "show vlan brief" is incorrect because the scenario requires: `show mac address-table` lists learned (and static) MAC-to-port mappings.

incorrect.explanation: "show cdp neighbors" is incorrect because the scenario requires: `show mac address-table` lists learned (and static) MAC-to-port mappings.

questions.question: How do you add a permanent (non-aging) MAC address entry to the table?
questions.explanation: `mac address-table static` manually binds a MAC to a VLAN and interface; it does not age out.

correct.explanation: `mac address-table static` manually binds a MAC to a VLAN and interface; it does not age out.

incorrect.explanation: "mac address-table aging-time 0" is incorrect because the scenario requires: `mac address-table static` manually binds a MAC to a VLAN and interface; it does not age out.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Static route next-hop vs exit-interface behavior

incorrect.explanation: "switchport mode static" is incorrect because the scenario requires: `mac address-table static` manually binds a MAC to a VLAN and interface; it does not age out.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Static route next-hop vs exit-interface behavior

incorrect.explanation: "arp <mac> static" is incorrect because the scenario requires: `mac address-table static` manually binds a MAC to a VLAN and interface; it does not age out.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Static route next-hop vs exit-interface behavior

answerReview.examTip: Next-hop static routes need a recursive lookup to find the exit interface.

questions.question: A brand-new server is plugged in and immediately receives a request from PC-A before it has sent any traffic. How does the switch deliver that first frame?
choices: It cannot — the server is unknown
It floods the frame (unknown unicast) since the server's MAC isn't learned yet
It drops the frame and logs an error
It sends an ARP request first
questions.explanation: Until the switch learns the server's source MAC, frames addressed to it are unknown unicast and get flooded.

correct.explanation: Until the switch learns the server's source MAC, frames addressed to it are unknown unicast and get flooded.

incorrect.explanation: "It cannot — the server is unknown" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Until the switch learns the server's source MAC, frames addressed to it are unknown unicast and get flooded.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "It drops the frame and logs an error" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Until the switch learns the server's source MAC, frames addressed to it are unknown unicast and get flooded.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "It sends an ARP request first" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Until the switch learns the server's source MAC, frames addressed to it are unknown unicast and get flooded.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

questions.question: A laptop is unplugged from port 1 and plugged into port 5 of the same switch. What happens to the MAC address table?
choices: Nothing — the entry stays on port 1 forever
The switch immediately deletes all entries
The old port 1 entry ages out (or is overwritten) and a new entry for port 5 is learned from the laptop's next frame
questions.explanation: Learning is continuous — the new frame from port 5 updates/relearns the mapping; the stale port-1 entry ages out.

correct.explanation: Learning is continuous — the new frame from port 5 updates/relearns the mapping; the stale port-1 entry ages out.

incorrect.explanation: "Nothing — the entry stays on port 1 forever" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Learning is continuous — the new frame from port 5 updates/relearns the mapping; the stale port-1 entry ages out.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "The switch immediately deletes all entries" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Learning is continuous — the new frame from port 5 updates/relearns the mapping; the stale port-1 entry ages out.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "The switch shuts down port 5" is incorrect because the scenario requires: Learning is continuous — the new frame from port 5 updates/relearns the mapping; the stale port-1 entry ages out.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

answerReview.examTip: Eliminate answers that describe a different protocol, port, or command than the stem asks for.

questions.question: How does a switch's forwarding behavior differ from a hub's?
choices: A switch forwards known-unicast frames to only one port; a hub repeats every frame out every port
A hub uses a MAC address table; a switch does not
A switch only works at Layer 3
questions.explanation: A hub is a simple repeater (everything out every port); a switch learns and forwards intelligently, only flooding when necessary.

correct.explanation: A hub is a simple repeater (everything out every port); a switch learns and forwards intelligently, only flooding when necessary.
